refuted.title: _release() 'idempotent / at-most-once' guarantee silently degrades to no guarantee if setting the per-future flag fails
refuted.why:
Refuted. The double-release the finding describes is unreachable, and the comment's idempotency guarantee holds for every input the function can actually receive.

(1) Every future that reaches _release originates from _pool.submit(fn) at seam.py:229, where _pool is a stdlib concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor (line 187, imported line 35). The two and only call sites — add_done_callback(_release) at line 246 and the explicit _release(future) at line 248 — both operate on that same stdlib Future. stdlib Future has no __slots__ and freely accepts arbitrary instance attributes, so the assignment future._assay_released = True at line 208 cannot raise AttributeError/TypeError. The except branch at line 209 is genuinely dead code, correctly marked `# pragma: no cover - Future allows attrs; defensive`.

(2) The PoC requires passing a future-like object with __slots__ (no _assay_released slot) or an assignment-blocking property to _release. No code path — public or internal — injects such an object; _release is only ever called with a stdlib Future. The finding itself concedes this is 'currently unreachable' and rates it low.

(3) On the residual 'comment overstates the guarantee' claim: the comment at line 209 explicitly documents that the assignment cannot fail for the actual type ('Future allows attrs') and that the except is purely 'defensive'. The 'released exactly once either way' / '_release runs at most once' claims therefore hold unconditionally over the entire realized domain of inputs. A guarantee that holds for every input a function can actually receive is, contractually, an unconditional guarantee; the only counterexample is an object the codebase cannot construct. There is no test, contract, or PoC demonstrating an actual _inflight over-release. This is a documentation nuance about provably-dead defensive code, not a defect.

refuted.title: OTLP exporter endpoint is not defended against OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_* env override (off-box span export)
refuted.why:
REFUTED. The finding's PoC (export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_ENDPOINT=http://attacker.example:4318 → off-box span export) does not work, for two independent reasons grounded in the actual phoenix.otel.register code path (verified by extracting the arize-phoenix-otel wheel source).

1) The wired exporter ignores the hostile env var. tracing.py:71 calls register(endpoint=tracing_endpoint(), ...) with an EXPLICIT loopback endpoint. In phoenix's register → BatchSpanProcessor(endpoint=...) → _normalized_endpoint(endpoint, ...): the OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT / PHOENIX_COLLECTOR_ENDPOINT env vars are read ONLY in the `if endpoint is None` branch (via get_env_collector_endpoint() in settings.py). With a non-None, scheme-bearing endpoint, _normalized_endpoint returns it verbatim and HTTPSpanExporter(endpoint=...) is constructed with the explicit value. The underlying OTel SDK OTLPSpanExporter honors an explicit endpoint= arg over the OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_* env vars (env is only the fallback when the arg is None). So spans go to loopback regardless of a hostile env value. This matches the test at tests/test_observability.py:46-53 asserting the endpoint is loopback and rejects non-loopback hosts.

2) The auto-instrumentation claim is architecturally false. LangChainInstrumentor (tracing.py:116, registered with set_global_tracer_provider=True at line 75) does NOT create its own env-reading OTLP exporter. Auto-instrumentation spans are emitted into whatever provider holds the global tracer slot — i.e. the exact TracerProvider register() built with the loopback BatchSpanProcessor. There is no second, env-var-derived exporter in the process. The instrumentor only produces spans; it does not configure export targets.

3) The "any third-party OTel SDK reading those standard env vars" path is speculative and absent: grep over src/ finds zero standalone OTLPSpanExporter/BatchSpanProcessor/TracerProvider construction, no opentelemetry-instrument auto-bootstrap, no sitecustomize, and no opentelemetry_instrumentor entry points in the package config. The only OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_* touch in the whole tree is os.environ.setdefault("OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_TIMEOUT", "2") (tracing.py:68), which never affects the endpoint.

Because the explicit endpoint= deterministically wins and there is no env-var-reading exporter anywhere in the process, an inherited OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_TRACES_ENDPOINT cannot ship spans off-box. The loopback enforcement on TRACING_HOST is sufficient here; no neutralization of OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_* is needed for span export to stay on-box. Caveat: this is correct only while the explicit endpoint= is passed (it is, and protocol is pinned to http/protobuf at line 74); a future regression dropping endpoint= would reopen the env fallback — but as written the concern is moot.

refuted.title: _release sets the idempotency flag on the Future under the lock but the done-callback may run before the flag attribute is settable, double-decrementing on AttributeError swallow
refuted.why: REFUTED. The PoC is unreachable in any real code path. _submit_bounded(fn) takes a callable, not a future; the future is always created internally by the module-level stdlib ThreadPoolExecutor via _pool.submit(fn) (seam.py:229). A caller cannot inject a future, and the code never subclasses/wraps Future, so the runtime type is always a real concurrent.futures.Future. The finding itself concedes that real Future allows arbitrary attribute assignment — so future._assay_released=True succeeds (seam.py:208), the guard getattr(future,'_assay_released',False) (line 205) flips True, and _release is genuinely idempotent; no double-decrement of _inflight occurs in production. Triggering the swallowed AttributeError branch requires a slotted Future-like double with no _assay_released slot, but (a) no such slotted Future subclass exists anywhere in src/ (only an unrelated _frozen.py dataclass uses __slots__), and (b) the only way to substitute such an object is to monkeypatch _pool.submit in a test — a self-contradictory, test-only artifact with no real-world counterpart. The existing test that exercises the exact double-release window (tests/test_persistence.py:737) uses a plain non-slotted _Fut double on which assignment succeeds and asserts _inflight stays 0 (not -1) after both the explicit fallback _release and the later callback fire. The except (AttributeError, TypeError): pass is genuinely dead defensive code (marked # pragma: no cover), not a reachable correctness hole. The 'at most once' invariant holds for every concrete future the system can actually produce.

refuted.title: HIGH_STAKES inner fail_after races the outer _with_timeout, and on cancellation parts/result_error are read after a partially-cancelled drain
refuted.why:
The finding is refuted. It bundles three sub-claims; none is a real defect in src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py.

(A) Timer race / ~30s extra slot hold: inner fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT) vs outer _with_timeout(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT + 30) is factually accurate, but the finding itself labels the extra slot hold "acceptable." The +30 IS the headroom margin, and the slot is bounded — _submit_bounded's saturation guard (lines 218-227) is the documented real protection, not the two-timer headroom (per the line 259-261 comment). Not a finding.

(B) Slot-leak re-introduction "if the SDK swallows the cancel": this is pure speculation about SDK misbehavior, not a code path. anyio.fail_after is a cancel scope with an exceeded deadline; once expired it re-delivers Cancelled at EVERY subsequent checkpoint within the scope, so a generator that swallows one Cancelled and immediately awaits again is cancelled again. Truly escaping requires a pathological anyio.CancelScope(shield=True) around an infinite await — not the SDK's contract. The PoC's "shielded sleep" is hypothetical and never demonstrated. The real behavior is covered and proven: tests/integration/test_reasoning_drain.py::test_t3_hung_subprocess_is_cancelled_not_leaked injects a _HANG (await anyio.sleep(3600)) and asserts the worker returns near the 0.3s inner timeout (< 5s), confirming fail_after cancels the hung stream and frees the slot (#127).

(C) The "bigger issue" — partial parts dropped on inner timeout — describes INTENDED, correct behavior, not a bug. On a timeout the ResultMessage never arrived, so the reply is incomplete/truncated. seam.py raises ReasoningError (lines 466-469) and discards parts deliberately, identical in spirit to the empty-reply guard (#R5, line 481). For a high-stakes VERDICT-ADJUDICATION path, returning truncated frontier text would corrupt downstream JSON parsing and verdicts — "salvaging" partial text is exactly what this path must NOT do. The ReasoningError is classified transient and RETRIED by _run_with_retries (line 579-583), so a near-complete reply that trips the timeout is re-fetched as a COMPLETE reply rather than surfaced corrupted. The finding frames correct, safety-preserving behavior ("retry instead of salvage") as a defect.

All three sub-claims are either self-admittedly acceptable, speculative about un-demonstrated SDK misbehavior, or a mischaracterization of deliberate correct design backed by passing tests. Refuted.

refuted.title: Bootstrap connection context-manager may COMMIT/return a connection left in a broken state if advisory-unlock and DDL both fail, with the pool reusing it
refuted.why:
REFUTED. The finding alleges a pooled bootstrap connection can retain the migration session advisory lock (because the best-effort unlock is swallowed and psycopg pools reset/reuse rather than close connections), thereby blocking a second process's pg_advisory_lock for the full lock_timeout. This cannot happen against real PostgreSQL + psycopg semantics; the PoC only achieves the retained-lock state by monkeypatching conn.execute to throw on the unlock statement — an injected fault with no realistic backing.

Decisive points (checked against src/assay_engine/persistence/checkpoint.py:283-296 and the pool config at 266-278):

1. The ONLY realistic way `SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(<constant>)` raises is a broken connection (network drop, server restart, backend OOM/crash). When a PG session terminates, PostgreSQL AUTOMATICALLY releases all session-level advisory locks held by that session. So at the very moment the failure that caused the raise occurs, the lock is already gone — there is no surviving session to block a second process. The "blocks for full lock_timeout" scenario is unrealizable.

2. `pg_advisory_unlock` on a valid constant bigint in an autocommit connection has no realistic server-side error path: it is a pure function, never waits (so lock_timeout is irrelevant to it), and has no constraint/serialization/deadlock failure mode. There is no transient error that returns an error response while keeping the lock held AND the connection healthy. The PoC's required state (unlock raises, lock still held, connection still reusable) is physically impossible without monkeypatching execute itself.

3. Defense in depth: the pool is built with check=ConnectionPool.check_connection (line 276), a liveness probe on every checkout that replaces dead connections — so even a broken bootstrap connection is never handed to a real checkpoint op.

4. The cross-referenced lock_timeout GUC leak (C-1) is session-local and cannot extend any OTHER process's advisory-lock wait; it is not the alleged blocking mechanism.

The finding's underlying observation — that psycopg3's default pool reset does NOT issue DISCARD ALL (only a conditional rollback), so the comment 'auto-releases when the connection returns to the pool' is imprecise — is technically accurate. But it is moot: the protective auto-release that actually guards the system is PostgreSQL's session-END release, which is real and sufficient for the only failure case (broken connection) where unlock can plausibly raise. The worst genuine artifact here is an imprecise comment plus an unconditional except-pass, not the cross-process serialization defeat the finding claims.

refuted.title: corpus_fingerprint materializes the full unit/relation payload before the 'streaming' hash, defeating the streaming optimization's intent
refuted.why: The finding's factual observations are correct — corpus_fingerprint (determinism.py:53-61) does materialize the full payload (a list of N unit dicts, each with a deep _plain()/canonical_plain copy of attributes, plus the relations list) into a single `payload` dict before `iterencode` is ever called, so iterencode does not bound peak RAM to O(1). However, the finding is refuted on its load-bearing normative claim, which is what determines whether it is a real defect: it asserts the docstring "overstates the benefit" and that the "streaming framing implies" O(1) / corpus-scale bounded RAM. The actual docstring (lines 48-51) claims neither. It states precisely: "streamed into the hash via iterencode rather than materialized as one giant string... this avoids holding the full serialized payload in RAM ON TOP OF THE ROW DICTS at hash time." The phrase "on top of the row dicts" explicitly concedes the row dicts (the materialized payload) are resident in RAM, and the benefit claimed is narrowly and accurately the avoidance of the additional full-size JSON *string* copy that json.dumps would build. That benefit is real and true: json.dumps would allocate a second O(payload)-sized object (the serialized string) on top of the already-resident payload dict, whereas iterencode emits chunks and updates the hash incrementally, never holding the whole string. So the optimization does exactly what the docstring says, and the docstring does not claim O(1) or corpus-scale bounding. The finding's PoC conclusion ("not O(1) as the 'streaming' framing implies") attacks an implication the docstring does not make — it misreads a scrupulously hedged docstring as a stronger claim than its words support. No code path is broken, no contract is violated, and the documentation is accurate as written; the finding does not identify a defect.

refuted.title: Unkeyed-trail warning and verify_trail=False fast path have no negative/behavioral test
refuted.why:
REFUTED. All three test gaps the finding claims are missing actually exist in /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/tests/test_pipeline.py and exercise exactly the affordances cited:

(a) UNKEYED warning fires on default path — `test_run_study_warns_when_trail_is_unkeyed` (line 679-688) runs run_study with no secret/trail under `warnings.simplefilter("always")` and asserts `pytest.warns(UserWarning, match="UNKEYED")`, directly pinning the line 300-305 warning.

(b) verify_trail=False skips the re-hash but still yields a valid result — `test_verify_trail_can_be_opted_out` (line 691-713) monkeypatches a counting spy onto `ProvenanceTrail.verify`, calls run_study with `verify_trail=False`, asserts the spy was NOT called (`calls["n"] == 0`), and then independently `verify_records(res.provenance)` confirms the produced chain is still intact. There is also a complementary positive test `test_verify_trail_default_still_verifies` (line 716-729) asserting the default invokes verify exactly once.

(c) secret+trail raises the documented ValueError — `test_secret_and_trail_are_mutually_exclusive` (line 747-754) asserts `pytest.raises(ValueError, match="either a caller-owned trail OR a secret")`, matching the guard at pipeline.py:291-292.

The finding's PoC ("grep of tests/ shows no assertion...") is factually wrong: grep over tests/ returns hits for UNKEYED, verify_trail (toggled both ways), and the mutual-exclusion ValueError. All test helpers referenced (auto_approve, verify_records, ProvenanceTrail, from_records) are imported/defined and the spied `verify()` method exists at provenance.py:202. Could not run pytest (no pytest module in this environment), but the test source is unambiguous and self-consistent. The safety affordances are covered; the test-quality gap does not exist.

findings.title: Post-hoc direction selection (HARKing) for whole-corpus hypotheses locked with predicted_direction=None
findings.description: _resolve_direction accepts a confirm-time predicted_direction whenever the locked hypothesis has predicted_direction=None, and nothing forces a WHOLE_CORPUS hypothesis to lock a non-None direction. A study can pre-register a directionless whole-corpus hypothesis, inspect the observed statistic/baseline, then choose 'greater' or 'less' at confirm_whole_corpus time to land in whichever tail is significant — textbook HARKing that defeats the pre-registration binding (issue #24 the docstring claims to close). Verified in source: when locked_dir is None and predicted_direction is None it raises, but when predicted_direction is supplied it is used as the resolved tail, so the verdict polarity is chosen post-hoc on a hypothesis whose cryptographic lock binds 'no direction'.
findings.poc: Lock a WHOLE_CORPUS Hypothesis with predicted_direction=None via lock_hypothesis (proof binds None). After observing obs=95.0 against a 0..99 null, call confirm_whole_corpus(..., predicted_direction='greater', authority=auth) -> 'supported'; had obs been low, pass 'less' instead. Pre-registration verification passes; tail chosen after seeing data.
findings.suggested_fix: Require predicted_direction at lock time for WHOLE_CORPUS hypotheses (raise in Hypothesis.__post_init__ when kind is WHOLE_CORPUS and direction is None), or in _resolve_direction reject locked_dir is None for any hypothesis going through require_preregistered (accept a confirm-time direction only for unlocked exploratory hypotheses). The directionless-deferred path must be unreachable for a pre-registered confirmatory test.

findings.title: Bootstrap lock_timeout session-GUC leaks into pooled connection used for all later checkpoint writes
findings.description: _acquire_migration_lock runs SELECT set_config('lock_timeout', %s, false) — is_local=false, i.e. SESSION scope (confirmed at source line ~210). The connection comes from `with pool.connection() as conn:` and is returned to the same pool on block exit; psycopg_pool does not reset session state without a reset= callback (none configured). That pool is then used by PostgresSaver for every subsequent checkpoint read/write, so the bootstrap-only lock_timeout (default 30s) silently persists on a long-lived pooled connection. Under lock contention a normal checkpoint write that would otherwise block briefly aborts with 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' — durability loss at exactly the interrupt/resume the checkpointer exists to guarantee.
findings.poc: With ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=30000, run get_checkpointer() to bootstrap, then re-check-out a connection and assert conn.execute('SHOW lock_timeout').fetchone() != '0'. Two concurrent resumes contending on the same checkpoint row produce a spurious 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' instead of completing.
findings.suggested_fix: Reset the GUC on the same connection before block exit after setup()/advisory-unlock (conn.execute("SELECT set_config('lock_timeout','0',false)") or RESET lock_timeout in finally); or configure ConnectionPool(reset=lambda c: c.execute('RESET ALL')); or run bootstrap on a dedicated one-shot psycopg.connect() that is closed rather than returned to the pool.

findings.title: BULK tier has no inner cancellation (asymmetric with HIGH_STAKES) — a hung backend permanently leaks a pool slot and drives endless retryable backpressure
findings.description: Confirmed in source: HIGH_STAKES wraps its stream in `anyio.fail_after(HIGH_STAKES_TIMEOUT)` (seam.py:438) so a hung subprocess is cancelled inside the worker and the bounded-pool slot is freed. BULK has no equivalent — it relies only on client_kwargs={'timeout': BULK_TIMEOUT} reaching httpx (seam.py:349) and the outer _with_timeout(..., BULK_TIMEOUT+10) (seam.py:510), which by its own docstring cannot interrupt a thread blocked in a backend call. If the httpx timeout does not fire (wedged stream, proxy holding the socket, ChatOllama not propagating client_kwargs, C-level blocking read) the worker is stuck forever and its _inflight slot never releases. _submit_bounded reports saturation as a RETRYABLE RateLimitError ('pool saturated', seam.py:223) and _run_with_retries treats it as transient backpressure, so once enough BULK workers wedge (up to _POOL_WORKERS=8) every future call livelocks on saturation backpressure that never clears.
findings.poc: Monkeypatch _bulk_complete to block with no timeout (threading.Event().wait()). Submit _POOL_WORKERS BULK requests via _with_timeout; each returns FuturesTimeout to the caller but the worker stays alive holding its slot. After 8, every _submit_bounded raises RateLimitError('saturated') indefinitely; slots never recover for the rest of process life.
findings.suggested_fix: Give BULK the same in-worker hard bound as HIGH_STAKES (run the generation under anyio.fail_after(BULK_TIMEOUT) via an async ollama client, or a cancellable subprocess). At minimum add a watchdog that surfaces a PermanentReasoningError ('reasoning pool wedged') on saturation that never clears rather than infinite RateLimitError backpressure, and assert ChatOllama honors client_kwargs timeout on the streaming path.

findings.title: unfreeze() promises 'stable order for reproducible output' for frozensets but the unorderable-element fallback iterates in non-deterministic order
findings.description: The unfreeze() docstring (line 146) states 'frozenset -> sorted list (stable order for reproducible output)' and the inline comment (line ~153) claims a 'stable repr'. Confirmed: the implementation tries sorted(value) and on TypeError falls back to [unfreeze(v) for v in value], iterating the frozenset directly. Frozenset iteration order for str elements varies with PYTHONHASHSEED and for FrozenDict/object elements with id-based hashing varies per run, so output order is NOT reproducible — contradicting both the docstring and the comment (the F-032 class). unfreeze() is the documented thaw for public payloads (GateReview.payload, #141) and determinism/provenance records that exist to be byte-for-byte reproducible, so non-deterministic ordering can break serialized-artifact comparison.
findings.poc: frozenset(['alpha',1,'beta',2,'gamma']) hits the fallback (mixed str/int unorderable); across PYTHONHASHSEED=0/1/2 unfreeze returns three different orderings of the same input. A frozenset of FrozenDicts (sorted raises TypeError) is similarly non-deterministic.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the fallback deterministic: return [unfreeze(v) for v in sorted(value, key=repr)] (or key=canonical_json(unfreeze(v)) for a fully type-faithful, process-stable key) and update the comment to say it sorts by a process-stable key.

findings.title: Contradicted verdict reported without any stability requirement, asymmetric with supported
findings.description: In confirm_whole_corpus a 'supported' verdict requires significance in the predicted tail AND stability >= threshold across resamples, but a 'contradicted' verdict is returned as soon as p_contra <= alpha, before stability is consulted, and stability is only ever measured in the predicted (support) tail. A single noisy observed statistic landing in the opposite tail yields a hard 'contradicted' with no reproducibility check, while the symmetric 'supported' claim is gated. In the §5 source-scoring path this means contradictions of external claims can be driven by non-reproducible noise, biasing alignment_rate downward (every unstable opposite-tail blip increments n_contradicted in SourceScorecard). The docstring frames this as intentional but it is a real methodological inconsistency: reproduction is demanded for one verdict polarity and not the other.
findings.poc: A null centered at 0; one observed draw lands in the opposite tail by chance. confirm_whole_corpus returns CONTRADICTED ('significant in the opposite tail') without measuring whether the contradiction reproduces across resamples, whereas the same magnitude in the supportive tail would be gated by stability_threshold; in adjudication it increments scorecard.n_contradicted.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply stability symmetrically: when p_contra<=alpha compute contra-tail stability and require it to clear the threshold before returning 'contradicted'; if resamples are absent return 'indeterminate (stability not assessed)' for the contradiction too. Otherwise document in METHODOLOGY.md why contradictions are held to a weaker bar and surface the missing-stability caveat in the verdict evidence.

findings.title: Direct confirm primitives default to a presence-only pre-registration gate (authority=None) — bypassable real-verification
findings.description: _gate_preregistration falls back to require_locked (a pure presence check on locked_at/timestamp_proof) when authority is None, and authority defaults to None on confirm_unit_level and confirm_whole_corpus. These primitives are study-facing (the adjudication docstring instructs studies to call them inside confirm callables). A study confirmer that calls them without threading the authority gets only the sentinel check, which a hand-written timestamp_proof='x' satisfies — no content-binding, no timestamp attestation, no lock-before-confirm ordering. In the composed run_study pipeline the runner pre-verifies via require_preregistered (defense-in-depth there), but any study/test driving these primitives directly, or a future runner that forgets to pass authority, silently downgrades pre-registration to a no-op.
findings.poc: Construct a Hypothesis, replace(locked_at='2020-01-01T00:00:00+00:00', timestamp_proof='not-a-real-proof'), then call confirm_whole_corpus(...) with authority omitted -> only require_locked runs -> the fake proof is accepted and a verdict is produced on a hypothesis whose lock was never cryptographically verified.
findings.suggested_fix: Make authority a required keyword argument on confirm_unit_level and confirm_whole_corpus (no None default) so every confirmatory call performs full require_preregistered. If an unverified exploratory path must exist, gate it behind an explicit loudly-named flag (verify_preregistration=False), forbidden for EXTERNAL_CLAIM/DISCOVERY origins.

findings.title: Operator username PII survives transcript scrubbing outside /Users path context (committed provenance leak)
findings.description: The scrubber's operator-username rule only matches the username directly after a home-path prefix: re.compile(r"(/(?:Users|home)/)[^/\s\"']+") (confirmed at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:51). Committed transcripts embed lsof/ps output where the username appears as a bare process-owner column (e.g. 'Claude 647 thomasjones 86u IPv4 ...'); that bare form is not preceded by /Users/ or /home/, so it is never redacted. The username appears ~570 times in non-path contexts in the tracked transcripts/sessions/*.jsonl. This defeats #F-024 ('committing transcripts does not publish the operator's identity') and the ADR-0003 data-sovereignty posture for a repo intended for public release; the same gap leaves any other PII surfaced by command output unredacted.
findings.poc: grep -oE '[A-Za-z]+ +[0-9]+ thomasjones' transcripts/sessions/*.jsonl prints lsof/ps lines the scrubber left intact; git ls-files --error-unmatch confirms the .jsonl is tracked/committed.
findings.suggested_fix: Redact the operator username as a standalone token: derive the username at runtime (Path.home()/source dir) and add a word-boundary pattern re.compile(rf"\b{re.escape(user)}\b") in addition to the path-prefix rule, so it is masked in process listings, git config, and env dumps. Re-run the scrubber over already-committed transcripts and amend, since the leak is already in history.

findings.title: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS and _POOLS_BY_CONN grow unboundedly — never pruned, keyed by raw conn_str
findings.description: _CONN_INIT_LOCKS is a process-lifetime dict keyed by resolved conn_str with add-only lifecycle (never removed); _POOLS_BY_CONN is the same. conn_str distinctness includes any URL variation (host alias, optional params, credential rotation that changes userinfo, sslmode toggles). A long-lived process targeting many distinct DSNs (multi-tenant runner, test harness rotating DSNs, credential rotation) accumulates a Lock per distinct string and, for _POOLS_BY_CONN, a live ConnectionPool + worker thread per distinct string with no eviction — an unbounded cache and thread/connection leak. Credential rotation makes the SAME logical DB appear as a NEW conn_str, orphaning the old pool (atexit-closed but never reused/closed until exit).
findings.poc: Loop setting ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL to N distinct valid loopback DSNs (e.g. differing in ?application_name=run_i) and call get_checkpointer() each iteration; len(_CONN_INIT_LOCKS) and len(_POOLS_BY_CONN) grow to N and worker-thread count climbs by one per distinct DSN, none reclaimed.
findings.suggested_fix: Canonicalize the cache key (drop volatile query params / strip userinfo for the key while keeping it for connect) so cosmetic/credential changes map to one entry; cap _POOLS_BY_CONN with an LRU that pool.close()es and removes the evicted pool from _OPEN_POOLS; prune _CONN_INIT_LOCKS for keys no longer in _POOLS_BY_CONN.

findings.title: run_study never persists or correlates the provenance trail to its MLflow run (success and failure paths)
findings.description: run_study records a hash-chained provenance trail and stores experiment_run_id inside the run_end entry (pipeline.py:721), but never writes the trail anywhere durable nor attaches it to the experiment run via tracker.log_artifact, even when a tracker and run_id are known. Persistence is opt-in (StudyResult.persist_trail() at :226 + a manual tracker.log_artifact). So the default governed run produces an MLflow run with metrics but no link back to the append-only trail, and the trail evaporates on process exit. The failure path is worse: the run_failed entry (pipeline.py:732) omits experiment_run_id even though run_id is in scope and the finally block terminates the MLflow run as FAILED (:754) — the case where correlation matters most leaves a FAILED run and an in-memory-only trail sharing no recorded id. GOVERNANCE §3 calls the trail 'the spine of reproducibility and of the independence claim'.
findings.poc: run_study(plan, gate_handler=auto_approve, tracker=MlflowExperimentTracker()): on success the MLflow run has params/metrics but zero artifacts and no provenance.json; StudyResult.provenance is gone on exit. With a discover() that raises, the run is set FAILED but run_failed has no experiment_run_id — given the FAILED run id you cannot find its trail, and vice versa.
findings.suggested_fix: When a tracker and run_id are present, have run_study persist the trail to a working path and tracker.log_artifact(run_id, path) on the success path (best-effort partial trail on failure), guarded like the other track() calls. Add experiment_run_id=run_id to the run_failed trail.record() payload (deterministic, preserves hash-determinism) so failed runs are equally correlatable across the trail and the experiment store.

findings.title: numpy resample-stability path allocates a full R×N boolean matrix (peak-memory blowup) and lacks an at-scale equivalence/memory test
findings.description: The numpy branch of _resample_stability computes null_arr[None,:] >= res_arr[:,None] (and the <= variant) before .sum(axis=1), materializing a dense R×N boolean array — O(R·N) PEAK MEMORY, whereas the pure-Python fallback it claims to merely speed up is O(N). Confirmed at confirm.py:216-217. At corpus scale (R=10k resamples vs N=10k permutation null = 10^8 booleans ≈ 100 MB transient; 20k×20k ≈ 400 MB) enabling the optional numpy extra silently raises peak memory by orders of magnitude and can OOM where the pure path would not, while the docstring frames it as 'purely a speed optimization, never a semantic change'. Compounding this, the only equivalence test (test_resample_stability_numpy_path_matches_pure_python, null=40/res=25) is far below any size where the mask is observable and pins no memory contract, so a future searchsorted/chunked rewrite has no at-scale guardrail.
findings.poc: null=list(rng.normal(size=20000)); res=list(rng.normal(0.5,size=20000)); confirm._resample_stability(res, null, 'greater', 0.05) transiently allocates ~400MB (a 20000x20000 bool mask), whereas the pure path's working set is O(N). No existing test exercises this size or any memory characteristic.
findings.suggested_fix: Sort the null once and use np.searchsorted for per-resample extreme counts in O((R+N)logN) time / O(R+N) memory: greater -> cnt = len(null) - searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, 'left'); less -> cnt = searchsorted(null_sorted, res_arr, 'right'); the (cnt+1)/(n+1)<=alpha test is byte-identical. If keeping the broadcast form, chunk the resamples and document the memory characteristic. Add a parametrized equivalence test at N=2000/R=2000 (and optionally a tracemalloc sub-quadratic peak-allocation regression test) so the rewrite is held to byte-identical results.

findings.title: discover_and_confirm does not type-guard the confirmer's return value (asymmetric with adjudication/pipeline)
findings.description: In adjudicate_with_baseline (adjudication.py:253) and the pipeline runner (pipeline.py:563) a confirmer that forgets to return (yields None) is caught with an explicit isinstance(verdict, Verdict) guard (#F-021). The standalone discovery runner was left out: after verdict = confirm(hypothesis, held_out) (discovery.py:150) it goes straight to verdict.hypothesis_id (:151, which only checks id-mismatch, not type). A confirmer returning None raises an opaque AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id' instead of the documented typed FirewallViolation. The module docstring (lines 94-97) promises FirewallViolation on a verdict<->hypothesis misattribution, and a None verdict never reaches that check, breaking the typed-exception contract for one of the two public Firewall-B entry points.
findings.poc: def bad_confirm(hyp, held_out): pass  # returns None. discover_and_confirm(corpus, split, discover=..., confirm=bad_confirm, authority=...) raises AttributeError("'NoneType' object has no attribute 'hypothesis_id'") instead of FirewallViolation('confirm returned NoneType ... expected a Verdict').
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror the adjudication/pipeline guard immediately after confirm(): if not isinstance(verdict, Verdict): raise FirewallViolation(f"confirm returned {type(verdict).__name__} for hypothesis {hypothesis.hypothesis_id!r} — expected a Verdict"). Verdict is already imported (line 39).

findings.title: from_records raises raw KeyError on a malformed record instead of the typed ProvenanceError
findings.description: from_records rebuilds ProvenanceEntry by directly subscripting each record (r['seq'], r['kind'], r['summary'], r['payload'], r['timestamp'], r['prev_hash'], r['entry_hash'] at provenance.py:266-275, confirmed). A record deserialized from an external/persistent store missing any key raises a bare KeyError, not the module's typed ProvenanceError. Every other deserialization/verification path here surfaces ProvenanceError (verify_records, _digest, clock/timestamp guards, freeze guard). The trail is explicitly a trust-boundary deserialization point ('rebuild entries ... and verify the chain'), so an externally-supplied record dropping/renaming a field should fail with the trail's typed error catchable alongside verify_records failures; a non-int seq / non-str field would also propagate untyped.
findings.poc: from_records([{ 'seq':0,'kind':'x','summary':'s','payload':{}, 'timestamp':'2020-01-01T00:00:00+00:00','prev_hash':'assay-provenance:v1:genesis' }]) -> KeyError: 'entry_hash' (expected ProvenanceError describing the malformed record).
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the construction in try/except (KeyError, TypeError, ValueError) as exc: raise ProvenanceError(f'provenance record could not be rebuilt: {exc}') from None, then call verify_records on the typed-good entries.

findings.title: test_cosine_matrix_scale_uses_fast_path passes even if the numpy fast path is reverted to the pure O(n^2*d) path
findings.description: The test claims to guard against 'a regression to the pure O(n^2*d) path at corpus scale', but its only fast-path-discriminating assertion is assert elapsed < 2.0; the rest (len==300, m[0][0]==1.0) hold for either path. The pure-Python _cosine_matrix_pure on a 300x64 matrix completes in ~0.08s — ~24x under the 2.0s threshold. Reverting cosine_similarity_matrix (primitives.py:155) to always call _cosine_matrix_pure leaves this test GREEN. The test does not discriminate the fix it names, and the loose wall-clock budget would not catch a real regression on a larger corpus either (and is flaky on loaded CI).
findings.poc: Change cosine_similarity_matrix (primitives.py:153-155) to unconditionally return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows); the test still passes (300x64 pure runtime ~0.08s < 2.0s, m[0][0]==1.0, dims 300x300).
findings.suggested_fix: Assert the fast path is taken rather than timing it: monkeypatch _cosine_matrix_pure to record/raise and assert it is NOT called when numpy is installed, or spy on _cosine_matrix_numpy and assert it WAS called. Drop the brittle non-discriminating elapsed < 2.0 assertion.

findings.title: test_install_flush_on_exit_wires_atexit_and_sigterm passes even if the SIGTERM handler wiring (#O3) is fully reverted
findings.description: The test asserts BOTH atexit and the SIGTERM handler are wired, but the SIGTERM half is asserted only inside `if "h" in captured:` (tests/test_observability.py:188-190). captured['h'] is populated solely as a side effect of the source calling signal.signal(SIGTERM, _handler) (tracing.py:141). If a developer reverts the SIGTERM block (tracing.py:131-141) keeping only atexit.register (:130), signal.signal is never invoked, captured stays empty, the if-branch is skipped, and the test passes on the atexit assertions alone. The SIGTERM portion of this regression guard is vacuous on the exact revert it is meant to catch — the conditional turns a should-be-required assertion into a no-op when the fix is missing.
findings.poc: Delete the `if threading.current_thread() is threading.main_thread(): ... signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, _handler)` block (tracing.py:131-141), keep atexit.register(lambda: _safe(flush)). The test still passes: atexit handler registered and flush>=1; captured is empty so the SIGTERM assertions are skipped.
findings.suggested_fix: Since pytest runs the body on the main thread, unconditionally assert 'h' in captured and exercise it: captured['h'](signal.SIGTERM, None); assert calls['shutdown'] >= 1. Remove the if-guard so a reverted handler fails the test.

findings.title: SBOM step re-runs pip-audit without the allowlist, so an accepted advisory fails CI
findings.description: The audit job's vuln gate (ci.yml:113-127) builds an IGNORES array from .pip-audit-ignore and passes --ignore-vuln to pip-audit. The next step 'SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile' (ci.yml:135) runs pip-audit ... --format cyclonedx-json under set -euo pipefail WITHOUT any --ignore-vuln args (confirmed). pip-audit exits non-zero on any found vulnerability regardless of output format, so any advisory the team has consciously allowlisted passes the gate step but FAILS the SBOM step — bricking CI on exactly the condition the allowlist exists to handle, and never emitting the SBOM when it is most needed. The allowlist is effectively a no-op once any accepted advisory is added.
findings.poc: Add a real advisory id (present for a requirements.lock-pinned package) to .pip-audit-ignore. The 'pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)' step passes (allowlisted); the 'SBOM' step runs pip-audit without --ignore-vuln, exits 1 on the same advisory, set -e aborts, the audit job fails, and all-checks blocks the merge.
findings.suggested_fix: Factor the IGNORES array into a shared function and reuse it in the SBOM invocation, or decouple SBOM generation from the vuln scan (cyclonedx-py requirements requirements.lock, or pass --ignore-vuln to the cyclonedx invocation). The SBOM should describe the dependency set independent of accepted advisories.

findings.title: Dependabot bumps pyproject ranges but cannot regenerate the hash-pinned lockfiles the CI gate enforces, so its PRs fail the lockfile-sync gate; uv.lock is unmanaged
findings.description: The pip ecosystem entry lets Dependabot bump pyproject.toml constraints, but the audit job (ci.yml:98-112) enforces that requirements.lock and requirements-core.lock are byte-for-byte regenerable from pyproject via uv pip compile. Dependabot has no hook to run uv pip compile, so any Dependabot PR changing a pyproject constraint leaves the committed lockfiles stale and FAILS the lockfile-sync gate -> all-checks blocks the merge -> the dependency-update PRs are unmergeable without manual intervention every time. Most runtime deps live in optional-extras with floors-only/range pins (langgraph>=1.2,<2), so Dependabot may not even open PRs while installed transitive versions drift. Additionally uv.lock is committed but referenced by nothing in CI (the gate compiles to requirements*.lock), an orphaned unverified lock artifact that can diverge unnoticed.
findings.poc: Wait for (or simulate) a weekly Dependabot pip PR bumping a pyproject constraint without regenerating locks. The 'Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject' step diffs requirements.lock against a fresh uv pip compile and exits 1 ('::error::requirements.lock is stale'); all-checks fails; the PR cannot merge.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add a CI/scheduled post-update step (or Dependabot-compatible action) that runs uv pip compile for both lockfiles and commits the result, or switch the supply-chain workflow to manage/audit uv.lock directly (uv-native) and drop the orphaned lock. Document which lock is canonical and enforce exactly one; consider versioning-strategy/grouping to batch lock regeneration.

findings.title: Unit-level confirm validates predicted_direction shape but never uses it, leaving direction_supports_claim unverified
findings.description: confirm_unit_level re-validates that hypothesis.predicted_direction is in {'greater','less'} as defense-in-depth, but the unit-level verdict is decided entirely by the trusted caller flag direction_supports_claim and never cross-checked against the pre-registered predicted_direction. A hypothesis locked with predicted_direction='greater' can be confirmed 'supported' on direction_supports_claim=True even when the held-out statistic points the other way — the engine binds a direction into the proof then ignores it at decision time. The validation at lines 162-168 gives a false sense the locked direction governs the verdict; it does not. Smaller than G-001 (requires a dishonest caller flag rather than free post-hoc choice) but the locked direction provides zero decision-time guarantee for the unit-level path.
findings.poc: Hypothesis locked predicted_direction='greater'. Caller passes direction_supports_claim=True with a statistic actually lower than baseline. verdict_from_pvalue returns SUPPORTED purely on the flag; the locked 'greater' is never consulted.
findings.suggested_fix: Require the caller to pass the observed comparison sign and derive supports = (observed_relation == locked_direction) inside the engine rather than accepting a free boolean. At minimum, when predicted_direction is locked, validate direction_supports_claim is consistent with caller-supplied directional evidence and record both in the verdict.

findings.title: scrubbed_env() omits known off-box redirect env vars the Claude CLI/SDK honors (extra-CA / redirect vectors)
findings.description: is_unsafe_subprocess_var() uses good shape rules for credentials/proxies (ANTHROPIC_*KEY/TOKEN, *_PROXY, AWS_*, GOOGLE_*) but the off-box-redirect set is a static allowlist of only three ANTHROPIC_*_BASE_URL names. Other documented transport-control variables the underlying node CLI / fetch stack honor are not covered — NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS and SSL_CERT_FILE (let an interceptor MITM egress with a rogue CA), and any future ANTHROPIC_* redirect that is neither a *_BASE_URL nor contains KEY/TOKEN (the docstring claims 'forward-proof' but the redirect half is a static list). Defense-in-depth: the loopback guard does not apply to the high-stakes off-box tier, so the env scrub is the only barrier for that subprocess.
findings.poc: Set NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS=/tmp/rogue.pem (or a future ANTHROPIC_* redirect var) before a HIGH_STAKES call; scrubbed_env() leaves it intact, so the child trusts the attacker CA / honors the redirect.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the off-box-redirect coverage shape-based to match the credential half: blank any ANTHROPIC_* and CLAUDE_CODE_* var except an explicit allowlist (CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN), and add NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS, SSL_CERT_FILE, SSL_CERT_DIR, REQUESTS_CA_BUNDLE, CURL_CA_BUNDLE to _UNSAFE_SUBPROCESS_VARS. Prefer an explicit allowlist for the subprocess env at this trust boundary.

findings.title: Gate.evaluate accesses decision.approved without confirming the precondition returned a GateDecision
findings.description: Gate.evaluate calls self.precondition(context) (gates.py:87) then immediately reads decision.approved (:88) and decision.reason (:89). precondition is a Callable on the adapter trust boundary; a study that returns a bool/None/any non-GateDecision (easy mistake: returning True instead of GateDecision.approved(...)) triggers an opaque AttributeError ('bool' object has no attribute 'approved') rather than the module's typed GateError. Same contract-asymmetry class pass 3 closed for confirmer returns (#F-021) but left open for gate preconditions. The module guarantees 'a transition is legal only if the gate returns an approved decision' with GateError as the documented failure type.
findings.poc: Gate(name='g', frm=..., to=..., precondition=lambda ctx: True).evaluate({}) -> AttributeError: 'bool' object has no attribute 'approved' (expected GateError).
findings.suggested_fix: After calling: if not isinstance(decision, GateDecision): raise GateError(f"gate {self.name!r} precondition returned {type(decision).__name__}, expected GateDecision"). Optionally wrap the call to normalize a raising precondition into GateError.

findings.title: make_gate_node re-raises a recorder failure after the interrupt resume was already consumed (stranded-resume bricking)
findings.description: In the gate node resume loop, once apply_gate_decision validates a resume value the node calls recorder(rec) (gatenode.py:145) inside the still-open while True loop, then returns. interrupt() (:137) has already consumed and persisted the operator's resume value into the checkpoint before validation. If recorder raises (trail clock raises, payload unserializable, provenance lock contended — all real ProvenanceError modes) the exception propagates out of node(). The module's own rationale (docstring 16-21, inline 133-135) is that a raise after the resume is consumed strands the value in the checkpoint and replays it forever, which is why a BAD resume re-fires interrupt instead of raising. A recorder failure on a GOOD resume hits exactly that hazard: the validated decision is lost, and on replay the node re-fires interrupt (already_decided False, recorder never wrote the terminal record), re-prompting the operator while the prior valid resume index is gone.
findings.poc: def flaky_recorder(rec): raise RuntimeError('provenance trail unavailable'). make_gate_node(gate, propose=..., recorder=flaky_recorder); operator submits a valid 'approved' resume; recorder raises -> exception escapes node(); the approved resume was already persisted by interrupt() and is lost on replay.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the in-loop recorder call resilient like the rest of the protocol: either attempt-record-then-return normalizing a recorder failure into a re-fired interrupt carrying the error, or record best-effort with a structured warning (mirroring pipeline.py:738-747 'never mask / never strand'). Do not let a recorder exception escape after interrupt() has consumed the resume value.

findings.title: build_baseline_artifact lets hash_value raise raw ValueError/TypeError on non-finite or unhashable extra_inputs
findings.description: At determinism.py:140 input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value) is called for every extra_input. hash_value -> canonical_json uses allow_nan=False and canonical_plain refuses non-JSON-native leaves, so a NaN/Inf float raises a bare ValueError ('Out of range float values are not JSON compliant') and an unhashable leaf raises a bare TypeError. preregistration.canonical_hypothesis_digest (preregistration.py:120-127) deliberately catches these and re-raises a typed, contextual PreRegistrationError naming the offending input; build_baseline_artifact — the other public consumer of the same canonical hashing — does not, so the determinism harness surfaces a low-context raw exception that does not identify which extra_input was bad.
findings.poc: build_baseline_artifact(corpus, {}, extra_inputs={'cfg': {'thr': float('nan')}}) -> ValueError: Out of range float values are not JSON compliant (no indication extra_input 'cfg' is the culprit).
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap per-input hashing: for name, value in (extra_inputs or {}).items(): try: input_hashes[name] = hash_value(value) except (ValueError, TypeError) as exc: raise ValueError(f"extra_input {name!r} cannot be canonicalized for a reproducible hash ({exc}); use JSON-native/finite values") from None.

findings.title: Audit-gate tooling (pip-audit, pip-licenses) is installed unpinned, undermining the reproducibility the job enforces
findings.description: The audit job pins uv to an exact version (ci.yml:103) for reproducibility and its whole purpose is a reproducible hash-pinned supply-chain scan, yet pip-audit is installed via python -m pip install --upgrade pip pip-audit (ci.yml:97, unpinned, no hashes) and pip-licenses via python -m pip install pip-licenses (:154, unpinned, confirmed). These tools run inside the trusted CI gate with checkout access; a malicious/broken release of pip-audit/pip-licenses (or a transitive dep) executes in CI and could suppress findings (a compromised pip-audit reporting clean) or break the gate — the same supply-chain risk class the job defends against, applied to the defender, and inconsistent with the uv pin.
findings.poc: A compromised pip-audit patch release publishes; the next CI run pulls it via --upgrade and runs attacker-controlled code with the lockfile and CI token in scope; a tampered pip-audit could exit 0 on a vulnerable set, silently passing the gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Pin pip-audit and pip-licenses to exact versions (matching the uv reproducibility posture), ideally from the hashed dev lockfile or with --require-hashes, adding them to the locked dev toolchain so they are covered by the same pinning/auditing.

findings.title: Pipeline builds the corpus unit-id set twice on the same corpus (redundant O(U) pass)
findings.description: In run_study, line 435 builds unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units} inside the DISCOVERY branch to validate split ids, and line 445 builds an identical corpus_unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units} unconditionally for the feature-builder ghost-id check (both confirmed). When a study runs in DISCOVERY mode AND declares feature builders (the common case) the corpus units are iterated twice to construct the same set. Cheap to fix and trivially correct.
findings.poc: A DISCOVERY-mode study with feature_builders triggers two full passes over corpus.units building the identical {unit_id} set (pipeline.py:435 and :445).
findings.suggested_fix: Hoist a single corpus_unit_ids = {u.unit_id for u in corpus.units} once before the DISCOVERY split check and reuse it for both the split-drift guard (436-441) and the feature-builder ghost-id guard (454-459); drop the duplicate comprehension.

findings.title: Comment claims the determinism freeze keeps an array 'opaque/O(1)' but freeze() copies the whole buffer via tobytes() (O(N))
findings.description: The docstring of cosine_similarity_matrix_array() (primitives.py:125) states 'the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1), unlike a nested list'. The actual freeze() path for an array-like (_frozen.py ~109-118) calls value.tobytes() and wraps it in a (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor, re-freezing that. tobytes() materializes/copies every byte — O(N) in elements, not O(1). The intended contrast is real (one tobytes() call rather than per-element Python recursion) but the literal 'O(1)' claim is false; both memory and time for freezing an array are O(N).
findings.poc: For an ndarray of n elements freeze() executes tobytes(), allocating/copying n*itemsize bytes — linear in n; a 10M-element array produces a 10M*itemsize-byte bytes object each freeze. 'O(1)' is therefore false.
findings.suggested_fix: Soften the comment to what is true: the freeze traverses an array in a single tobytes() pass (O(1) traversal depth / no per-element Python recursion) but the conversion is O(N) in array size. Replace 'opaque/O(1)' with 'opaque, frozen in a single tobytes() pass (no per-element recursion), unlike a nested list'.
